ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00KY covers biscuits and cookies as a category; chocolate cookie is a member of this group.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one chocolate cookie weighing approximately 30 g, providing around 145 kcal, 6.5 g fat, and 20 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Cookie, chocolate chip — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F11080014 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are preserved from the HPB source. Iron is reported as 0.0 mg/100g by HPB; this is a confirmed zero per the source and is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Cookies and Biscuits — University of Sydney GI Database; chocolate-flavoured cookie analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 40 is consistent with published University of Sydney GI Database values for chocolate cookies and shortbread-type biscuits (GI range 35–44 for similar products). Glycemic load per 30 g serving calculated as: (40 × 66.54 × 0.30) / 100 = 7.98 g. The input record cited GI 40 from an embedded GI database; this is corroborated by University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for chocolate biscuit/cookie products. GL per serving recalculated from first principles using HPB carbohydrate value.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (29.4 g/100g) and moderate sodium (275 mg/100g) with no compensating potassium data
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid chocolate cookies: the high sugar content (29.4 g/100g) promotes insulin-driven sodium retention and raises blood pressure independently of dietary sodium, while the 275 mg/100g sodium adds further cardiovascular burden; limit to occasional very small portions only.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar (29.4 g/100g) and high total carbohydrate (66.54 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (2.38 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chocolate cookies are unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: sugar at 29.4 g/100g far exceeds the 15 g/100g threshold, total carbohydrate is 66.54 g/100g with only 2.38 g fibre, and even a single 30 g serving delivers a glycaemic load of approximately 8 g — contributing meaningfully to postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (21.71 g/100g) with estimated saturated fat ~11.5 g/100g, high sugar, and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chocolate cookies pose a high cardiovascular risk due to elevated saturated fat (~11.5 g/100g from butter), high sugar (29.4 g/100g) which raises triglycerides and LDL, and moderate sodium (275 mg/100g); individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should treat this food as an occasional indulgence only and keep portions to one small cookie.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (~11.5 g/100g) and high sugar (29.4 g/100g) are pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chocolate cookies are unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis: saturated fat from butter (~11.5 g/100g) exceeds the RA threshold and does not qualify for the MUFA/PUFA carve-out, and sugar at 29.4 g/100g promotes inflammatory mediators; avoid or consume only rarely.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A chocolate cookie is a sweet, baked snack made from wheat flour, sugar, butter, cocoa powder or chocolate, eggs, and leavening agents. The dough is portioned and baked until the exterior is set with a slightly crisp edge while the interior remains tender and chewy. The flavour is rich, sweet, and chocolatey, with a dense, crumbly-to-chewy texture depending on the butter-to-flour ratio and baking time. Chocolate cookies are widely consumed globally as a snack or dessert item and are commercially produced at scale. Per 100 g, they deliver 484 kcal, 21.71 g fat (predominantly saturated from butter), 66.54 g carbohydrate, 29.4 g sugar, 6.41 g protein, 2.38 g dietary fibre, and 275 mg sodium. The high sugar and refined carbohydrate content make chocolate cookies unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The glycaemic index is approximately 40 (low GI), but the glycaemic load per 30 g serving is approximately 8 g, which is clinically meaningful when multiple cookies are consumed. Calcium is present at 36 mg/100g from dairy ingredients. Iron is reported as 0.0 mg/100g per the HPB Singapore database. This food is classified as ultra-processed and carries high-fat and high-sugar dietary warnings.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a chocolate cookie?
How much sugar is in a chocolate cookie?
Is chocolate cookie high in fat?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. GI value of 40 is corroborated by University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for chocolate cookies. Saturated fat estimated at ~11.5 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central data for comparable chocolate cookies (USDA SR Legacy: Cookies, chocolate chip, commercially prepared, regular, higher fat, enriched) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat; this is documented in notes.
notes: 1. Iron is reported as 0.0 mg/100g by HPB Singapore Foodo for this record (cr_id F11080014). This is a confirmed zero per the approved source. 2. Saturated fat (11.5 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 'Cookies, chocolate chip, commercially prepared, regular, higher fat, enriched' (NDB No. 18158), as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item. This is an adapted value and is flagged accordingly. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific HPB chocolate cookie record are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026. This unavailability is confirmed by consulting the HPB Foodo database directly. 4. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate corroborated by University of Sydney GI Database entries for chocolate biscuit/cookie products (GI range 35–44); the input embedded GI database value of 40 is consistent with this range. Glycaemic load per 30 g serving recalculated as 7.98 g. 5. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; multilingual local-name fields are not applicable and are set to null. Alias coverage is English-only as appropriate for a Western generic food item. 6. Halal status is assumed true for generic commercial chocolate cookies without pork-derived emulsifiers; this should be verified against specific product labelling for packaged variants.
